name: SLB N.V.

profile.description: SLB N.V. (formerly Schlumberger Limited) is the world's largest oilfield services company, providing technology for the energy industry worldwide through four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. The company also develops carbon management, geothermal, and adjacent energy technology. Rebranded from Schlumberger to SLB in October 2025. Founded in 1926, headquartered in Houston, Texas.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB executed a significant corporate transformation — rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB in October 2025 to signal its pivot from oilfield services to a global energy technology company [2]. The company expanded into carbon capture (SLB Capturi), geothermal, and hydrogen technologies while completing the ChampionX acquisition to strengthen its production chemicals business. These represent genuine new revenue streams beyond legacy oilfield services. AI Deployment (1/2): SLB's digital annual recurring revenue surpassed $1B, driven by the DELFI cognitive E&P environment and Lumi AI platform for subsurface analytics [1]. CEO Le Peuch has emphasized AI-powered operations as central to SLB's strategy. However, $1B digital ARR is still under 3% of total revenue. Market Share (1/2): SLB is the #1 global oilfield services company by revenue ($35.7B FY2025) with dominant international market position. However, international revenue declined 4% in Q1 2026 due to Middle East disruptions in Qatar and Iraq [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): DELFI and Lumi platforms, SLB Capturi carbon capture technology, and OneSubsea subsea production systems are in active development with revenue visibility [2]. R&D Intensity (1/1): SLB invests ~$700M+ annually in R&D (~2% of revenue), leading the oilfield services sector, with significant patent portfolio across drilling, completion, and digital technologies [2].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): SLB's rebranded vision of 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' is specific and intentional — it connects the company's work to the energy transition while maintaining relevance in oil and gas [2]. It's clearly been thought through as part of the rebranding, differentiating SLB from peers. However, the vision hasn't yet been validated through a full cycle of crisis-tested execution under the new identity. Community Impact (1/2): SLB has sustainability programs across 109,000 employees in 100+ countries, with community investment and workforce development initiatives. The company's SBTi-validated emissions targets demonstrate genuine environmental commitment [3]. Specific charitable dollar amounts were not prominently featured. Employee Retention (1/2): Glassdoor reviews describe SLB as 'innovation-focused' with 'cutting-edge projects' but also note a 'performance-driven, high-pressure work environment' [2]. CEO Le Peuch's 'Living Our Culture' program aims to improve internal culture. The 109,000 global workforce has typical oilfield services cyclical turnover patterns. Insider Buying (1/2): Insider ownership is 0.22%, typical for a company of this size. No significant net insider buying identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on dividend stock metrics. FY2025 revenue was $35.7B with Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B (3% YoY growth) [1]. D/E of 42.5% is well below the 70% target. Forward P/E of 16.7 is below the 20 target. ROE of 14.1% is just below the 15% target. Profit margin of 9.3% is the primary weakness — well below the 20% target, reflecting oilfield services' structurally lower margins. Payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target but manageable. SLB's dividend was cut in 2020 from $2.00 to $0.50 annually and has been growing since — 5 consecutive years paid at new level, 4 years of growth, with impressive 18.7% 5yr CAGR from the reduced base. Free cash flow of $3.0B supports the $1.18 dividend and $2.4B planned buybacks [1]. Revenue growth YoY of 2.7% and 5yr CAGR of 8.6% show moderate but steady recovery from COVID lows. Liquidity of $932M daily turnover clears the $1M floor.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): SLB's core business involves physical-world operations — drilling wells, managing reservoirs, deploying subsea production systems, and manufacturing oilfield equipment — that AI structurally cannot displace [1]. These services require heavy equipment, specialized field crews, and execution in extreme environments (deepwater, high-pressure/high-temperature). The regulatory and safety requirements of global energy operations create additional defensive moats. AI enhances SLB's offerings (subsurface modeling, drilling optimization) but cannot replace the physical services. The only modest exposure is that AI-optimized operations could reduce the total number of service runs needed. AI Opportunity (1/1): SLB's digital annual recurring revenue surpassed $1B, driven by the DELFI and Lumi AI platforms providing cloud-based subsurface analytics and operational optimization [1]. This represents quantifiable, growing AI-driven revenue — though still under 3% of total revenue, it demonstrates a clear AI monetization pathway in energy technology.

infinite_game.summary: SLB under Olivier Le Peuch is the most infinite-game-oriented oilfield services company. The rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB, the pivot toward energy transition technologies (carbon capture, geothermal, hydrogen), and the $1B+ digital ARR milestone demonstrate genuine long-term strategic thinking. The 'Living Our Culture' program shows intentional investment in organizational resilience. However, the company's core revenue remains deeply tied to fossil fuel demand, and the 2020 dividend cut revealed vulnerability to commodity cycles.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): 'Driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' is a genuine and specific purpose that connects SLB's work to a larger vision — the energy transition as both opportunity and responsibility [2]. The rebranding from Schlumberger was a deliberate strategic signal, and investments in carbon capture (SLB Capturi), geothermal, and hydrogen show the purpose is being operationalized. However, the Just Cause hasn't been tested through a full crisis cycle under the new identity — the company is only months into its rebranded vision. Oil and gas still generates >95% of revenue, creating tension between the stated cause and economic reality.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Le Peuch demonstrated courage by rebranding a 100-year-old company, investing in energy transition technologies during a period of oil industry resurgence, and acquiring ChampionX to build production chemistry capabilities [1][2]. The company maintains meaningful R&D investment in low-carbon technologies even when they don't generate near-term returns. However, the 2020 dividend cut was a reactive decision driven by the COVID crisis, not a proactive long-term bet, and the core business strategy remains focused on maximizing oil and gas returns.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Le Peuch's 'Living Our Culture' program represents deliberate investment in organizational trust and psychological safety across 109,000 employees in 100+ countries [2]. Glassdoor reviews describe innovation-focused work and cutting-edge projects, suggesting intellectual engagement. However, the oilfield services industry's cyclical nature (SLB cut thousands of jobs in 2020) structurally undermines long-term trust, and reviews also note 'high-pressure' performance expectations that can create stress rather than safety.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): SLB's transformation from Schlumberger — the world's largest oilfield services company — into 'SLB, a global energy technology company' represents a genuine attempt at existential flexibility [2]. The company is investing in carbon capture (SLB Capturi), geothermal, hydrogen, and digital platforms that could sustain the business beyond peak fossil fuel demand. The rebrand itself — dropping the Schlumberger family name after 100 years — signals willingness to fundamentally redefine the company's identity. This is the energy services industry's closest analog to NVDA's gaming-to-AI pivot, though it's still early and unproven.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB management frames competition with Halliburton and Baker Hughes as motivation to innovate rather than a war to win. Le Peuch's strategic language focuses on 'driving innovation' and 'redefining energy' rather than 'beating competitors' [2]. The company's expansion into adjacent markets (carbon capture, digital, geothermal) reflects a learning orientation that uses competitive dynamics to identify white space rather than fighting for incremental market share.

investment_thesis: SLB is the world's largest oilfield services company at $35.7B revenue with an unmatched global footprint, transitioning into a broader energy technology platform under CEO Olivier Le Peuch [1]. Q1 2026 revenue grew 3% to $8.72B, digital ARR surpassed $1B, and the company guided $36.9-37.7B for FY2026 with $4B+ shareholder returns planned (including $2.4B buybacks and a 3.5% dividend increase). D/E of 42.5% is conservative, Forward P/E of 16.7 is reasonable, and the 2.12% dividend yield provides income. The energy transition strategy — carbon capture, geothermal, hydrogen — positions SLB for long-term relevance as the only oilfield services company genuinely building beyond hydrocarbons.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on execution and valuation compression. EPS declined 24% in FY2025 to $2.38, and Q1 2026 net income fell 6% YoY despite revenue growth, reflecting margin pressure from Middle East disruptions in Qatar and Iraq [1]. International revenue declined 4% in Q1, concerning for a company that generates 74% of revenue outside North America. Profit margin of 9.3% is structurally low. The PEG ratio of 1.91 suggests the stock is not cheap relative to growth. Energy transition investments (carbon capture, geothermal) remain pre-revenue or early-revenue and may take years to contribute meaningfully — SLB Capturi and NewEnergy ventures still represent <1% of revenue. The 2020 dividend cut from $2.00 to $0.50 demonstrates that commodity cycle risk can rapidly destroy income quality, and consecutive years of dividend growth is only 4 years from the reduced base.
key_catalysts: Digital ARR surpassing $1B with DELFI and Lumi AI platforms gaining traction — digital services offer structurally higher margins than traditional oilfield services and could drive margin expansion above 10%
Middle East and offshore deepwater spending recovery driving international revenue re-acceleration beyond the Q1 2026 dip, with $36.9-37.7B FY2026 revenue guidance implying 3-6% growth

Energy transition portfolio (SLB Capturi carbon capture, geothermal, hydrogen) reaching commercial scale, potentially opening a new $5B+ addressable market by 2030 that diversifies away from hydrocarbon dependence
key_risks: International revenue declined 4% in Q1 2026 due to Middle East disruptions — geopolitical instability in Iraq, Qatar, and other key markets could create sustained headwinds for SLB's largest revenue base
EPS declined 24% in FY2025 despite stable revenue, indicating margin erosion from pricing pressure and operational inefficiency that may worsen if commodity prices decline further

Energy transition investments remain largely pre-commercial — if carbon capture, geothermal, and hydrogen fail to scale, SLB's rebranding narrative loses credibility while the core oilfield services business faces secular demand risk from peak fossil fuel scenarios
